name: NVIDIA Cosmos 3
tagline: The physical-AI omnimodel that made license nerds sit up
description: A unified world model for physical AI: video generation, physical reasoning, and action generation in one architecture. Ships as Nano (8B reasoner + 8B generator) for efficient inference and Super (32B + 32B) for large-scale synthetic data, with a cookbook, training framework, and post-training scripts across the Cosmos ecosystem.
why: Top of the robotics benchmarks, and the license is the other story: NVIDIA moved Cosmos to the Linux Foundation's OpenMDW-1.1 — a genuinely permissive model license granting rights 'without restriction' — rather than its own restriction-laden Open Model License.
useCases: Synthetic data generation for robot and AV training
Physical reasoning over video for embodied agents
Post-training world models for specific robots and environments
audience: Robotics teams, AV researchers, simulation and synthetic-data engineers

setup.requirements: Nano wants a serious GPU; Super is datacenter territory. The ecosystem spans several repos — start from the Cosmos cookbook

license.notes: Editor's judgment call, flagged for review. OpenMDW-1.1 (Linux Foundation) is MIT-grade in substance: rights granted 'without restriction' across copyright, patent, database, and trade-secret law; attribution-only conditions; no use or output restrictions; a litigation-termination clause. But it is not OSI-approved, and our bright line is OSI approval — so per house policy we downgrade rather than round up. Substantively among the most permissive model licenses in use.

limitations: Datacenter-class hardware for the Super variants, an ecosystem sprawling across a dozen repos with mixed licenses and generations, and OpenMDW itself is young — permissive on paper, untested in court.
take: When the biggest chipmaker on earth picks a brand-new license for its crown-jewel models, the license is the news. This one reads better than almost any corporate model license before it.
